name: Python
scope: An individual at BASIC level, with 0-2 years of experience, understands Python syntax and basic programming concepts like variables, data types (integers, floats, strings, booleans), and operators (arithmetic, comparison, logical). They can use control flow statements (if, elif, else) and loops (for, while) to solve simple problems. They are capable of writing simple scripts to automate basic tasks, perform calculations, and handle file I/O operations like reading from and writing to text files. They can work with fundamental data structures such as lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets, and can define and call functions with arguments and return values. They know how to import and use standard library modules (e.g., math, random, datetime) and handle exceptions using try and except blocks. They use basic tools like text editors or simple IDEs (IDLE, Visual Studio Code), run scripts from the command line, and manage packages with pip. They can debug simple syntax and runtime errors using print statements, read and understand straightforward code written by others, and make minor edits to existing scripts under guidance. They are not expected to design complex systems but can follow predefined specifications and contribute to projects by handling straightforward tasks.

name: Llamaindex
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in Llamaindex as a competency should be able to set up a Python ≥ 3.8 environment with pip/Conda, virtualenv, Jupyter/VSCode, install llama-index 0.8+, OpenAI SDK, FAISS or Chroma, sentence-transformers, Streamlit/Gradio, Git and optional Docker, and manage API keys via dotenv or AWS Secrets Manager. They can ingest small-to-moderate corpora using SimpleDirectoryReader, WebPageReader, PDFReader, NotionPageReader, attach metadata, split text with default TextSplitter adjusting chunk_size and overlap, and create Document and Node objects. They construct and persist VectorStoreIndex, ListIndex or SummaryIndex on in-memory, FAISS or Chroma stores through ServiceContext defaults, reload them from disk, and explain Index vs VectorStore vs Retriever vs QueryEngine. They build a retrieval pipeline by instantiating Retriever or QueryEngine, tuning PromptHelper parameters like top_k, temperature, max_tokens, context_window and executing sync/async queries that return answer, source_nodes and similarity scores. They understand cosine similarity, embedding model choice (text-embedding-ada-002, sentence-transformers-all-mpnet), token counting, chunk granularity’s effect on recall, and basic system/user prompt templates. They perform lightweight evaluation using precision@k/recall@k, log latency, token usage and cost, and resolve errors such as rate limits, missing embeddings or serialization issues. They can wrap query calls into FastAPI/Flask endpoints, Streamlit/Gradio UIs or LangChain agents, exchange data via JSON or Pydantic schemas, version code in GitHub and script idempotent index regeneration. They apply best practices: keep secrets out of VCS, record hyper-parameters and results, prevent trivial prompt injection and cap OpenAI expenditure. They deliver reproducible demos that achieve roughly >60 % precision@5, persist indexes locally and provide clear README hand-offs. They are not expected to design custom retrievers, hybrid or multi-index graphs, fine-tune embeddings, optimize latency at scale, build advanced RAG evaluation, observability or high-availability production systems.
long_scope:
Competency: Llamaindex  
Proficiency Band: BASIC (≈ 1–2 years focused use)  
Scope Definition: Everything a practitioner at BASIC level must reliably know, do, and care about when applying Llamaindex in production-adjacent or PoC settings.

───────────────────────────────────────────
1. Core Deliverables & Outcomes
───────────────────────────────────────────
• Produce a working end-to-end pipeline that:  
  – Ingests small-to-moderate, mostly text-based corpora (a few MB to tens of MB)  
  – Builds a single, default index (Vector / Summary / Keyword Table) using packaged readers and chunkers  
  – Exposes a simple query interface (command line, Jupyter or minimal API endpoint) returning coherent natural-language answers with citations  

• Demonstrate predictable retrieval quality by adjusting a handful of first-order hyper-parameters (chunk size, similarity top-k, temperature, model choice) and validating that changes behave as expected.

• Keep operational hygiene: store API keys securely, persist the index locally, version notebooks/scripts, and document set-up steps clearly for hand-off to teammates.

───────────────────────────────────────────
2. Day-to-Day Tasks They Are Expected To Perform
───────────────────────────────────────────
• Environment preparation  
  – Install Llamaindex and required back-ends (e.g., OpenAI python package, FAISS, Chroma) via pip or Conda  
  – Configure environment variables, secrets and basic logging

• Data ingestion & preprocessing  
  – Use built-in readers (SimpleDirectoryReader, WebPageReader, PDFReader, NotionPageReader, etc.)  
  – Apply default text splitting / chunking logic; adjust chunk size & overlap for different document types  
  – Convert raw input into Document and Node objects and persist them via JSON-based storage or simple vector stores

• Index construction  
  – Build VectorStoreIndex, ListIndex or SummaryIndex with default ServiceContext  
  – Swap between in-memory, FAISS or Chroma vector stores through simple constructor parameters  
  – Persist and reload indexes to/from disk

• Query pipeline  
  – Create QueryEngine or Retriever objects with defaults  
  – Manipulate PromptHelper and QueryConfig to control answer length, top_k, and streaming  
  – Run synchronous or async queries in Python; capture answer, source_nodes, similarity scores

• Minimal evaluation & troubleshooting  
  – Use built-in retriever evaluations (precision @k, recall @k) on small labelled sets  
  – Log latency, token counts & cost for OpenAI calls; identify dominant cost drivers  
  – Identify and resolve common errors: rate limits, schema mismatches, missing embeddings, serialization issues

• Integration touch-points  
  – Wrap Llamaindex query calls inside a FastAPI/Flask endpoint, Streamlit UI or LangChain agent  
  – Use Pydantic models or plain JSON for request/response schemas

───────────────────────────────────────────
3. Foundational Concepts They Must Command
───────────────────────────────────────────
Retrieval Concepts  
• Difference between “indexing” and “retrieval” within Llamaindex  
• Cosine similarity over embedding vectors; effect of embedding model choice  
• Chunk size vs. semantic granularity and its impact on recall

Llamaindex Building Blocks  
• Document, Node, Metadata, ServiceContext, PromptHelper, LlamaPromptTemplate  
• VectorStore vs. Index vs. Retriever vs. QueryEngine separation of concerns

LLM & Embedding Basics  
• Role of OpenAI GPT-3.5/4 or local LLMs as text generators  
• Role of embedding models like text-embedding-ada-002 or sentence-transformers-all-mpnet  
• Token counting, context window limitations, temperature, max_tokens

Persistence & Storage  
• Simple JSON storage, pickled Python objects, VectorStore persistence (FAISS index files / Chroma SQLite)  
• Trade-offs of in-memory vs. disk storage

Prompting Essentials  
• Structure of default question-answer prompts  
• System vs. user messages mapping inside Llamaindex templates  
• High-level awareness of prompt injection and basic mitigation (e.g., context limiting)

───────────────────────────────────────────
4. Tools, Libraries & Platforms They Should Be Comfortable With
───────────────────────────────────────────
Python >=3.8, pip/Conda, virtualenv, Jupyter / VSCode Notebooks  
Llamaindex 0.8+ public API surface  
OpenAI Python SDK (key management, usage tracking)  
FAISS or Chroma (basic indexing, saving, loading)  
Sentence-Transformers for optional local embeddings  
Streamlit or Gradio for lightweight demos  
Git for version control, GitHub/GitLab for repository management  
Dotenv / AWS Secrets Manager / environment variables for key storage  
Basic shell scripting and JSON/YAML editing  
(Optional) Docker for encapsulating dependencies

───────────────────────────────────────────
5. Quality & Best-Practice Awareness
───────────────────────────────────────────
• Source documents should carry metadata (file path, URL, last-modified) for traceability.  
• Index and embedding regeneration should be scripted and repeatable (idempotent builds).  
• API keys must never be committed to VCS; rely on .env or secrets store.  
• Handle user input defensively to avoid prompt injection, runaway costs or TOS violations.  
• Document default hyper-parameters and rationales; record experimental results for reproducibility.  
• Follow semantic version pinning to prevent breaking changes during team hand-offs.

───────────────────────────────────────────
6. Boundaries of BASIC Proficiency
───────────────────────────────────────────
Is NOT yet expected to:  
• Design custom Retriever classes, complex hybrid search, or multi-index composability graphs  
• Tune embeddings or train domain-specific models from scratch  
• Architect high-availability, production-grade deployments with autoscaling and observability  
• Optimize retrieval latency at scale through sharding, caching, async batch embedding pipelines  
• Implement sophisticated RAG evaluation frameworks, guardrails or fine-grained access control policies

───────────────────────────────────────────
7. Evidence of Mastery
───────────────────────────────────────────
A practitioner at BASIC proficiency will consistently deliver small working prototypes that:  
• Run in a fresh environment from a README without manual fixes  
• Answer domain questions with >60 % precision@5 on a labelled test set  
• Keep total OpenAI cost within a defined budget via sensible context sizing  
• Are accompanied by concise documentation explaining data flow, dependencies and next steps for scaling

In an interview, a candidate operating at this level should confidently walk through each segment above, articulate trade-offs, and demonstrate hands-on competency through a pair-coding or white-boarding exercise that mirrors these requirements.
